definition: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units called tokens that can be processed by a language model.
Byte Pair Encoding is a subword tokenization algorithm that iteratively merges frequent character pairs into tokens.
WordPiece is a subword tokenization algorithm used in BERT that splits words into smaller meaningful units.
SentencePiece is a language-independent tokenization algorithm that treats text as a sequence of Unicode characters.
An embedding is a dense vector representation of text or data that captures semantic meaning.
Word embedding is a numerical vector representation of words that preserves semantic relationships.
A transformer is a neural network architecture that uses attention mechanisms instead of recurrence or convolution to process sequences.
Attention is a technique that allows models to focus on the most relevant parts of the input when making predictions.
Self-attention allows each token in a sequence to attend to every other token in the same sequence.
Cross-attention allows tokens in one sequence to attend to tokens in another sequence.
Multi-head attention uses multiple attention mechanisms in parallel to capture different relationships in the data.
Positional encoding provides information about token order to transformers which otherwise process tokens in parallel.
A large language model is a neural network trained on massive text datasets to understand and generate human language.
Pretraining is the process of training a model on large unlabeled datasets before fine-tuning on specific tasks.
Fine-tuning is the process of adapting a pretrained model to a specific task using labeled data.
Masked Language Modeling trains models to predict missing words in a sentence.
Next Sentence Prediction trains models to determine whether two sentences appear sequentially in the original text.
Dropout is a regularization technique that randomly disables neurons during training to prevent overfitting.
Overfitting occurs when a model learns training data too well and performs poorly on new unseen data.
Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple to capture patterns in the training data.
An epoch is one complete pass through the entire training dataset during model training.
Batch size is the number of training samples processed before the model updates its parameters.
Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm used to minimize a model's loss function.
Stochastic Gradient Descent updates model parameters using one training sample at a time.
Adam is an adaptive optimization algorithm that combines momentum and RMSProp.
A loss function measures the difference between predicted outputs and actual target values.
Perplexity measures how well a language model predicts text, with lower values indicating better performance.
BLEU score evaluates machine translation quality by comparing generated text with reference translations.
Precision is the ratio of true positive predictions to all predicted positives.
Recall is the ratio of true positive predictions to all actual positives.
F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall.
A confusion matrix is a table used to evaluate classification performance.
Vanishing gradients occur when gradients become too small during backpropagation.
Exploding gradients occur when gradients become excessively large during training.
A neural network is a computational model inspired by biological neurons that learns patterns from data.
Deep learning is a subset of machine learning that uses multi-layer neural networks.
Machine learning is a field of AI that allows computers to learn patterns from data.
Supervised learning trains models using labeled data.
Unsupervised learning discovers patterns in unlabeled data.
Reinforcement learning trains agents to make decisions through rewards and penalties.
A reward function defines the feedback signal used in reinforcement learning.
A policy defines the strategy an agent uses to choose actions.
A vector database stores embeddings and allows efficient similarity search.
Semantic search retrieves documents based on meaning rather than exact keyword matches.
Dense retrieval uses embeddings to find semantically similar documents.
BM25 is a ranking function used in information retrieval to score documents based on keyword matches.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a method where language models retrieve external knowledge before generating answers.
Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller pieces for embedding and retrieval.
The context window is the maximum amount of text a language model can process at once.
Hallucination occurs when a language model generates incorrect or fabricated information.
Prompt engineering is the practice of designing prompts to guide model outputs.
Instruction tuning trains models to follow natural language instructions.
Chain-of-thought prompting encourages models to reason step by step.
Graph of Thoughts structures reasoning paths as graphs rather than linear chains.
An AI agent is a system that perceives its environment and takes actions to achieve goals.
A multi-agent system consists of multiple interacting agents solving tasks collaboratively.
Zero-shot learning allows a model to perform tasks it has never explicitly been trained on.
Few-shot learning trains or prompts models to generalize from a very small number of examples.
One-shot learning enables models to learn from a single example per class.
In-context learning allows language models to learn from examples provided directly in the prompt without weight updates.
A prompt is the input text given to a language model to guide its output.
A system prompt is an instruction given to a language model that defines its behavior and role.
Temperature controls the randomness of a language model's output, with higher values producing more diverse responses.
Top-p (nucleus) sampling selects tokens from the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds p.
Greedy decoding selects the highest probability token at each step during text generation.
Beam search explores multiple candidate sequences simultaneously during decoding to find higher quality outputs.
A tokenizer converts raw text into tokens that can be processed by a language model.
The vocabulary is the complete set of unique tokens a language model can recognize and generate.
Logits are the raw unnormalized scores output by a model before applying softmax.
Softmax converts logits into a probability distribution over all possible output tokens.
Layer normalization normalizes activations within a layer to stabilize training.
Residual connections add the input of a layer to its output to help gradients flow during training.
A feed-forward network in a transformer applies two linear transformations with a non-linear activation between them.
The encoder in a transformer processes input sequences and produces contextual representations.
The decoder in a transformer generates output sequences from encoder representations.
An encoder-decoder model uses an encoder to understand input and a decoder to generate output.
An autoregressive model generates text one token at a time conditioned on all previous tokens.
A causal language model predicts the next token given all preceding tokens in a sequence.
BERT is a bidirectional transformer model pretrained on masked language modeling and next sentence prediction.
GPT is a family of autoregressive language models developed by OpenAI trained on next-token prediction.
LLaMA is a family of open source large language models developed by Meta AI.
Mistral is a family of efficient open source language models developed by Mistral AI.
A RAG pipeline combines document retrieval with language model generation to produce grounded answers.
Reranking re-scores retrieved documents using a more powerful model to improve relevance ordering.
A cross-encoder jointly encodes a query and document together to produce a relevance score.
A bi-encoder encodes queries and documents independently into vectors for fast retrieval.
FAISS is a library by Meta for efficient similarity search over dense vector embeddings.
Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors to determine their semantic closeness.
Dot product similarity computes the scalar product of two vectors as a similarity measure.
Hybrid search combines dense vector search and sparse keyword search to improve retrieval quality.
Reciprocal Rank Fusion merges ranked lists from multiple retrieval systems by combining their reciprocal ranks.
A knowledge base is a structured repository of information used to support question answering systems.
Grounding connects language model outputs to real world facts or retrieved documents to reduce hallucination.
In RAG systems citation refers to attributing generated answers to specific source documents.
A document loader reads and parses files such as PDFs or web pages into structured text for processing.
A text splitter divides long documents into smaller chunks suitable for embedding and retrieval.
Chunk overlap preserves context across adjacent chunks by repeating a portion of text between them.
Sparse retrieval uses keyword based methods like BM25 to find relevant documents.
Mixture of Experts routes inputs to specialized subnetworks to improve model efficiency and capacity.
Quantization reduces model size and inference cost by using lower precision numbers for weights.
Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to mimic the behavior of a larger teacher model.
